planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun; clary sage is a biennial that grows vegetatively in its first year, then blooms spectacularly in its second year, after which it typically dies but self-seeds freely to maintain the colony.

directions: Salvia sclarea is a majestic biennial sage with large, wrinkled, woolly, gray-green leaves and tall (4-5 foot), airy panicles of pale lavender-blue and white flowers with large, colorful bracts in early summer of the second year that are spectacular in the garden and highly attractive to bumblebees; it is one of the most valuable aromatic plants for the perfume industry (used in Eau Sauvage and Chanel No. 19), for aromatherapy (clary sage essential oil), and as a culinary herb for flavoring wines and beers. The large leaves can be dipped in batter and fried as a traditional Italian dish; the essential oil is considered antispasmodic and is used in herbal medicine.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 4-6 weeks before) in well-drained, average to poor, neutral soil in full sun; painted sage is a drought-tolerant, heat-loving annual that blooms best in lean soil with minimal fertilizer, and deadheading extends the display.

directions: Salvia viridis (syn. S. horminum) is an annual Mediterranean sage grown primarily for its striking, colored bracts — terminal clusters of papery, veined, pink, purple, or white leaf-like structures surrounding tiny, insignificant flowers — that provide vivid color for 2-3 months in summer and are outstanding both in the garden and as dried flowers (the colored bracts hold their color well when dried and are superior to many everlastings). The plant has a mild sage-like fragrance and has some historical use as a beer flavoring. The cultivar series 'Oxford Blue', 'Pink Sunday', and 'White Swan' are the most widely grown ornamental selections.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun; summer savory is a fast-growing annual that prefers dry, lean conditions — rich, moist soil produces excessive soft growth with poor flavor.

directions: Satureja hortensis has been cultivated as a culinary herb since antiquity, known in Latin as the herba satureia, and is the essential companion to bean dishes of all kinds — dried or fresh leaves added to green beans, shell beans, lentils, and legumes reduce flatulence, enhance flavor, and are a traditional pairing in European cuisine; in France it is known as poivre d'âne (donkey's pepper) and in Germany as Bohnenkraut (bean herb). The small, narrow, dark-green leaves have a peppery, thyme-like flavor more pungent than winter savory (S. montana) and are outstanding for seasoning sausages, stuffings, and fresh cheeses. An excellent companion planting for beans and an important pollinator resource for bumblebees and honeybees.

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost in dry, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun; common thyme must have excellent drainage to survive humid summers — in the deep South, grow it as a cool-season perennial (fall through spring), as it tends to die out in zone 9 summer heat and humidity.

directions: Thymus vulgaris is the primary culinary thyme of European and world cuisine — a low, bushy, semi-woody perennial with tiny, aromatic, gray-green leaves containing thymol and carvacrol, the essential oil compounds responsible for its powerful, distinctive fragrance. It is the definitive herb of French, Italian, Spanish, and Middle Eastern cooking; an essential component of bouquet garni and herbes de Provence; and one of the most important medicinal herbs in Western herbalism, used for respiratory infections, coughs, and as an antimicrobial. The tiny, pale-lavender flowers in late spring are outstanding for honeybees and are the source of highly prized thyme honey.

planting.note: Plant rooted slips (not seeds) 2-3 inches deep and 12-18 inches apart after soil warms to at least 65°F in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic, low-nitrogen soil in full sun; avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers which promote vine growth at the expense of root tubers.

directions: Ipomoea batatas is a tropical American vine domesticated in Central or South America before 2500 BCE and now grown worldwide as one of the most nutritious staple vegetables — the orange-fleshed varieties are among the richest plant sources of beta-carotene, vitamin C, potassium, and dietary fiber. Grow from slips (rooted stem cuttings) purchased in spring or sprouted from a store-bought tuber in a jar of water; harvest after 90-120 frost-free days when vines begin to yellow, digging carefully to avoid cutting the roots. In zone 9-10, slips left in the ground may overwinter and produce a perennial planting; cure harvested tubers at 85-90°F for 1-2 weeks before storage to convert starch to sugar and heal surface cuts.
